column1: Piezometers record subsurface pore-water pressures at different depths and locations within
the slide mass.  Pressure at a particular depth is shown as an equivalent water depth, in
centimeters. Groundwater in an open tube, slotted at the depth of the sensor, would rise to this height above the sensor.  Sensors
are temperature-compensated,  amplified, strain-gauge type pressure transducers vented to the
atmosphere.  All pressure transducers are amplified ~190X.  Piezometer construction diagrams show details of installations.
Extensometers record downslope displacement of the slide ground surface at different locations
throughout the slide.  Extensometers use a linear position transducer and extendable metal
cables.  One end of the cable is anchored on stable ground and the other on potentially moving
ground. Extensometer cables, above the ground surface, are oriented approximately parallel to
the local ground surface; directions of the cables are shown on sensor location maps.  Extensometers values are reset
to zero on October 1 of each water year, so values are cumulative for the water year.
Rain gauge records precipitation, including rainfall and snowmelt.  The gauge is a "tipping spoon"
type that records in units of 0.01 inch and values are converted to mm.  The site receives
modest amounts of snow that may last a few days.  Snow is captured in the gauge and recorded as
precipitation as it melts.  Precipitation is reset to zero on October 1 of each water year, so
values are cumulative for the water year.
